professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how digital platforms reshape labor markets this semester. Many food-delivery and ride-hailing companies classify their workers as independent contractors rather than employees, which keeps prices low but leaves workers without benefits like paid sick leave. Some economists argue this flexibility is exactly what many workers want, while others insist it simply shifts risk onto the people least able to bear it. Should governments require gig platforms to treat their drivers as full employees? Why or why not?

students.name: Marco
students.text: I think reclassification is overdue. Treating drivers as contractors lets companies externalize costs any conventional employer would absorb, and the workers bearing that risk rarely chose it freely. If a platform depends entirely on these drivers to operate, it should shoulder the corresponding obligations rather than quietly offload them.

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Mandatory employment status sounds protective, but it could backfire. Many drivers genuinely value setting their own hours and would lose that autonomy under rigid schedules. Rather than forcing one model, I'd prefer portable benefits that follow workers across platforms, preserving flexibility while still addressing the real gap in protections.

professor.name: Dr. Lin
professor.text: We've been discussing how software gets built at scale this semester. A growing number of companies now rely heavily on AI coding assistants that generate large blocks of code from a short description. Some engineers argue these tools free developers to focus on design and the harder conceptual problems, while others worry that teams are shipping code no one fully understands. Do you think companies should limit how much AI-generated code goes into critical systems? Why or why not?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I'd support clear limits for critical systems. When code is generated faster than anyone can review it, subtle defects slip into exactly the places where failures are most costly. Understanding precisely why a system behaves as it does is part of engineering, and outsourcing that comprehension entirely to a model feels reckless.

students.name: Jared
students.text: Hard limits seem premature. The real issue isn't where code originates but whether it's tested and reviewed properly. A well-designed pipeline catches faulty machine output the same way it catches human mistakes, so I'd invest in stronger verification and accountability rather than capping a genuinely useful tool.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: More and more streaming services have begun using detailed viewing data to decide which shows get renewed and even how individual episodes are structured. Supporters say this finally gives audiences more of what they demonstrably enjoy, while critics warn it pushes creators toward safe, formulaic content that algorithms predict will perform well. Should studios let audience data play a major role in creative decisions? Why or why not?

students.name: Zoe
students.text: I'm wary of letting metrics steer storytelling. Data describes what audiences already liked, not what might surprise or genuinely move them, so optimizing for it tends to reward imitation. Some of the most memorable shows would never have tested well in advance, and a purely data-driven model quietly filters those out.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: To be fair, ignoring audience data seems wasteful. Studios invest enormous sums, and viewing patterns reveal which ideas truly connect rather than which merely sound impressive in a pitch meeting. Used as one input among several, data can reduce expensive misfires without dictating every creative choice a writer makes.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been examining how cities try to encourage healthier eating this semester. Several municipalities have introduced taxes on sugary drinks, using the revenue to fund nutrition programs in schools. Some public-health experts argue these taxes meaningfully reduce consumption, while others contend they burden lower-income families without changing long-term habits. Do you think cities should rely on taxes like these to improve public health? Why or why not?

students.name: Helen
students.text: I think targeted taxes can work when the revenue is reinvested visibly. Price signals do shift everyday purchases, and directing the money toward school nutrition addresses the habits that form earliest. The policy isn't merely punitive if families can see tangible benefits returning to their own communities and children.

students.name: Phil
students.text: My concern is that these taxes hit the households with the least flexibility hardest. A small price increase rarely uproots deeply established habits, but it does strain tight budgets. I'd rather see cities invest directly in affordable fresh food and education, which tackles the cause instead of the symptom.
